back_translation: The LORD visited Sarah as He had said, and the LORD did to Sarah as He had spoken.
Sarah conceived and bore a son to Abraham in his old age, at the appointed time of which God had spoken.
Abraham gave the name Isaac to the son who was born to him, whom Sarah bore to him.
When Isaac his son was eight days old, Abraham circumcised him, as God had commanded him.
Abraham was a hundred years old when his son Isaac was born to him.
Sarah said, "God has made laughter for me; everyone who hears will laugh with me."
She added, "Who would have said to Abraham, 'Sarah will nurse children'? Yet I have borne him a son in his old age."
The child grew and was weaned, and Abraham held a great feast on the day Isaac was weaned.
But Sarah saw the son of Hagar the Egyptian, whom she had borne to Abraham, mocking.
She said to Abraham, "Cast out this slave woman and her son, for the son of this slave woman shall not inherit with my son, with Isaac."
The matter was very grievous in Abraham's eyes because of his son.
But God said to Abraham, "Do not let it be grievous in your eyes about the boy and your maidservant. Whatever Sarah says to you, listen to her voice, for through Isaac shall your seed be called.
And also the son of the slave woman I will make into a nation, because he is your offspring."

Abraham rose early in the morning, took bread and a skin of water and gave them to Hagar, placing them on her shoulder, along with the boy, and sent her away. She went and wandered in the Wilderness of Beersheba.
When the water in the skin was finished, she cast the boy under one of the bushes.
She went and sat down opposite, far away, about a bowshot, for she said, "Let me not see the death of the boy." She sat opposite, lifted up her voice, and wept.
God heard the voice of the boy, and the angel of God called to Hagar from heaven and said to her, "What is wrong, Hagar? Do not fear, for God has heard the voice of the boy where he is.
Get up, lift up the boy, and hold his hand, for I will make him into a great nation."
God opened her eyes, and she saw a well of water. She went and filled the skin with water and gave the boy a drink.
God was with the boy, and he grew up and lived in the wilderness and became an archer.
He lived in the Wilderness of Paran, and his mother took for him a wife from the land of Egypt.
At that time Abimelech and Phicol the commander of his army said to Abraham, "God is with you in everything you do.

Now therefore, swear to me here by God that you will not deal falsely with me or with my offspring or my descendants. According to the kindness I have shown you, you shall do to me and to the land in which you have sojourned."
Abraham said, "I will swear."
Abraham reproached Abimelech concerning a well of water that Abimelech's servants had seized.
Abimelech said, "I do not know who did this thing. You did not tell me, and I myself have not heard of it until today."
Abraham took sheep and cattle and gave them to Abimelech, and the two of them cut a covenant.
Abraham set apart seven ewe lambs of the flock by themselves.
Abimelech said to Abraham, "What are these seven ewe lambs which you have set apart?"
He said, "You shall take these seven ewe lambs from my hand, that it may be a witness for me that I dug this well."
Therefore that place was called Beersheba, because there the two of them swore an oath.
They cut a covenant at Beersheba, and Abimelech and Phicol the commander of his army arose and returned to the land of the Philistines.
Abraham planted a tamarisk tree at Beersheba, and there he called on the name of the LORD, the Eternal God.
Abraham sojourned in the land of the Philistines for many days.
